sentences.text: No, we had no problem with food.
Food we had, until the end.
Because Slovakia is rich enough, food.
We had enough food.
The problems were that we already know that we are people that our neighbors don't like.
They were slowly, like I said before, adapted.
Even though we were only two or-- two, three Jewish families on the street.
The Jewish problem was a daily problem everywhere.
When we went to stores, they didn't liked us.
I don't know why.
We looked exactly like they looked.
We were dressed exactly the same they were.
No.
The yellow star we started to wear only when the Germans came in-- in April, 1944.
Until then?
Well, first of all, they knew us for so many years.
You have between 18,000, 20,000 population, 2,000 Jews, that they were born there.
Their parents were born there.
Not by the name, but they know exactly.
People live together so many years, so they know exactly who are the Jews.
And we went to the public swimming pools.
They didn't want us to be with them, together.
Not because they didn't liked us.
We had very good looking boys and very pretty Jewish girls.
And they liked to be between the Jews.
But because their positions, they wanted to show off, that they don't want Jews around them.
So this was a tough, tough two or three years, very bad, in the school especially.
We had fights with the-- with the children and with the teachers.
Did-- do you think the-- the children, the teenagers, handled this situation differently than the adults?
Did you feel somehow rejected?
Well, yes.
Because you know, children are more cruel than the-- than older people, probably, just by-- by calling names.
But the older people were doing the job.
The children, they were just yelling, dirty Jew.
But the older people, they did what they were told to do.
And slowly, like I said before, the Jewish life almost slowly, slowly disappeared.
And we couldn't go to the-- our Jewish gatherings that we went before.
And it was very bad.
No, he had to work in his business, and more than before.
Although his partners were fair, I think.
I don't think he had any problem with them, maybe he didn't told us.
But he was working hard.
I don't think so.
No.
Yes.
One of the partners was Lawrence.
That-- he died during the war.
No.
Family, Lawrence.
And he died during the war, as a soldier.
The other partner, I don't remember.
I don't remember.
But I am happy about it, that I don't remember their names.
I just don't want to remember them at all.
Because I have hard feelings.
I-- I-- how is it possible that when we were walking from the ghetto towards the train station, 3,000, 4,000 Jews, and the Gentiles were standing from the both sides of the street and watching.
I didn't see no one that was crying.
Maybe the people, they were crying, they were some older people inside, in their homes.
So I don't know, probably.
But none of them I saw crying.
People that we lived together for so many years, and we had so close relationship.
I just can not understand how they turned so cold-blooded in these few years.
So I am mad at them.
No, no.
The men-- when I was walking out, I felt ashamed.
As a little boy, I remember my backpack.
I had a backpack.
Everybody had something else in their hands.
But I had a backpack.
And I feel terrible, humiliated.
And if I would see the reactions that they feel sorry for us, probably I wouldn't be-- I wouldn't have this humiliated feeling.
But I felt humiliated.
I don't know how they felt, my father or my brother or my mother.
But personally, I felt terrible humiliated.
Sure, of course.
Yes.
Yeah.
I had a bar mitzvah in the ghetto.
Yeah.
So when-- when we were ordered to take our belongings from the-- from this army complex-- not the ghetto anymore-- but we were in this army complex.
We were walking towards the train station.
It wasn't too far, maybe a kilometer, a kilometer and a half.
But this long line of Jews, walking with their belongings in fairly hot weather, but with the backpacks and the luggages and with the winter coat.
It-- end of May.
Or beginning of June.
Beginning of June.
I don't remember the day.
But I remember that it was a couple days after the D-Day.
Because in this army complex, in this ghetto, we got newspapers. 'Invasion,' I remember it.
And it was June 6th, 1944.
It was two or three days later.
Can you believe it?
(WHISPERS) Give me a minute.
Yes.
Yes.
It was the invasion.
And the Americans were already on the European soil.
And nobody cared that they are taking.
I know that, by then, already millions died.
But this was the last million, almost.
That didn't have to die.
That didn't have to die.
Now I know exactly that it could have.
I'm sure that Roosevelt was able to prevent.
Because the trains, the railroads, were bombed all over.
My city's situated from Auschwitz, maximum four hours' train ride, five hours' train ride.
We were leaving Levice.
Took us five days all over through Hungaria, through Slovakia, through the Carpath Mountains to cross the border to Poland.
Why?
Because the railroads were bombed.
Bridges were destroyed.
But the railroad to Auschwitz was intact.
So this is the reason that-- [CRYING]
Yes, of course.
Yeah.
I don't think they tried to.
It's not because they didn't want to.
But they were helpless.
They just didn't know what is happening.
We were trying to get together-- I mean, stay together.
We were in the boxcars, about 20 or 25 from my immediate family.
We were from the city.
Cousins and uncles.
Well, some of the uncles, they went-- were taken to the working camps in Hungaria, and they died.
None of them came back.
But we were about 20 people [? that ?] [? made it. ?]
About 50 people in the boxcar.
It was a cattle car.
About 50 people, yes.
About 20 of them were our close family, yes.
Well, it was my aunt or uncle and my-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] aunt Rosa.
And my-- no, aunt Helen was in-- in Budapest.
And was my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Well, my-- my cousin, Magda, that I saw her even in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the [?
Pryor ?] family, which was from my-- Grandma-- from my mother's side.
The father, we called him [?
Yenner Bachi ?], the uncle [?
Yenner ?] and his son, his daughter, Vera.
She lives in Israel.
His wife-- forgot, right now, her name.
And so on and so on.
I-- I can't remember exactly the names anymore.
But about 20 we were, in this cattle car.
By the way, my father was in charged with the cattle car.
Every-- every boxcar had a-- in charge.
Because he was a soldier.
So he knew what to do.
When the-- when the train stopped, the Hungarian and the Germans, they called us.
They were giving, during the five days-- four days' travel, we got bread and onions.
The green onions, they gave us.
But we had food with us.
Because from the ghetto we took, in the luggages, food.
So food was no problem.
Water was probably a problem in the boxcars.
But food was enough.
So--
We were thirsty.
The train stopped.
They used to bring, in buckets, water.
But, no, food was OK.
It was very overcrowded.
We couldn't-- we were hardly were able to lay down at night, one next to each other.
But the worst part was the sanitation.
There was only a bucket, like a toilet.
And-- you know, 50 people, to use a bucket overnight when the boxcar is closed.
It was-- it was a terrible experience.
It was summer.
June.
Well, it wasn't too hot.
The weather was OK.
But the 50 people locked in a boxcar, you know.
So we were all trying to reach these four tiny barbed-wire windows.
And we did it by turns.
Everybody, few minutes, you know, to get some fresh air.
But when the train stopped, every day the train stopped.
Then they opened the-- the-- the doors.
Mhm.
And we were able to go to the bathroom under the-- under the boxcars.
Well, it was terrible frightening.
Yeah, the boxcars were very frightening.
Because the older people, that-- they understand more than we did.
It was, for them, hard to believe that we are going to some nice place in those ugly boxcars.
But we-- we had strong feelings that, yes, they are taking us somewhere into a-- relocate, to relocate us deep in Hungaria.
I mean, we were sure.
My-- the father, I remember, told us.
Because the-- the-- the train made certain unusual turns from Levice to Budapest.
Then from Budapest back to Slovakia.
We didn't know why it's making all those routes.
Now I know that's because the railroads were bombed all over.
But we thought that maybe they are looking for the right place for us.
So who knows?
So in June '44, the Jewish community still didn't know that there were gas chambers and--
No.
No.
No.
No.
Only when we crossed the Slovakian and Polish border-- my father was well-traveled, like I said-- we heard trucks.
He-- he saw the Polish name from a town.
And I remember he was looking out.
And he said, we are in Poland.
The whole 50 people, we started to cry.
This was to us, something unbelievable.
What we are doing in Poland?
Maybe then they understand, the older people, that those stories that the little children told us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this-- this is the-- this is the story.
Why, otherwise, would take us to Poland?
So all the horror stories that they were not telling us children, but they knew about it.
Probably then they realized that those are the true stories.
Do you know what the town was that your father saw?
I don't remember the town.
It was a small town.
I don't remember the name, because it had no significance for me.
But when the train crossed the border, my father said that he's sorry that we didn't run away because we had a-- we were offered to ran away from the ghetto.
Two families, the [?
Bandler ?] family, we were very closed.
My father came at night to ghetto.
And he said that we made arrangement to run away to Slovakia, back to Slovakia.
He had very good connection in Slovakia.
And the people, the Gentiles, probably, they know already in there, the problems.
I mean, of course they know.
And for some reason, we decided not to run away.
My father had lot of friends in Slovakia.
No, the [?
Bandlers ?]-- it was another Jewish family that we were together.
Like, absolutely no family connection.
But we were growing up together.
The-- one of the boys-- I mean, one of the-- his son, the [?
Bandlers' ?] son, went together with my brother to school.
Their sister was a little bit older.
She's-- she survived the concentration camps.
She lives in Israel.
Her name is Alice.
Now it's Noye.
But it used to be [?
Bandler. ?]
She lives in Israel.
Yes.
Yes.
In the ghetto, we-- we had like, a three or four-bedroom apartment.
Everybody-- the entire family had one room.
But we shared the kitchen from this building.
Yes.
We considered.
No.
No, no.
They died just like our family.
I survived.
From their family, Alice survived.
She was older.
She came back home.
I think she was 18.
Four, maybe.
I think it was four nights and five days, something like this.
Took us to Auschwitz.
Middle of June.
Well, the train traveled in Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We crossed the border, I think, early in the morning.
And by afternoon we were in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the name of the station.
It was written, 'Auschwitzen.' It was a gray, tall building.
When I looked out from the window, it didn't mean too much to us.
Because nobody, I think nobody, knew what is Auschwitzen.
It was a tiny little town.
Who knows?
But I saw the name.
Everybody saw that we are in Auschwitzen, the train station.
And the train slowed down, slowly.
But then it passed the train station.
And the train was traveling another 10 minutes probably, or 15 minutes.
Very, very slow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- the train stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was afternoon, late afternoon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, it-- the train stopped, and it was very quiet.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, all the doors from the entire convoy, I think there were 40 or 42, or even more.
The train was very, very long.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The doors, those heavy wooden doors, at once, they opened.
And the yellings from these strange-looking people.
They jumped.
The different language-- [GERMAN], out!
The-- the-- the-- the-- everything sounded like you-- you are in a madhouse.
Everything was so-- so unbelievable.
Fast, fast.
Out.
Out.
[GERMAN], from the-- from the boxcars.
So we were jumping.
And we were trying to help the older people to get off from the train.
And they pushed us away, those strange-dressed people.
That they were prisoners.
Polish prisoners, that they were, already, many years.
They wore the blue and gray-striped dress with this round hat.
And they looked terrible.
We didn't know that they are Jewish.
Well, they looked like animals.
They behaved like animals.
And they were just ordering us, very quick, to leave the-- the boxcars.
And we were trying to catch our belongings.
And they said, you don't need it.
They were yelling in Yiddish and Polish and Hungarian, all the different languages.
But fast.
Fast.
Fast.
This was very, very scary.
This was probably the scariest moment in my life 'til then.
It was very scary.
And-- and they told us to separate, to-- the children here with the mothers and the-- the older people.
So my father, he was a young man.
43 years old.
39 years old.
So my mother grabbed my little brother, Gabi.
And my father grabbed me from the one side, and my older brother from the other side.
He says, you are coming with me because you're a bar mitzvah boy already.
And probably this is the reason I'm here today.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] And we saw that they are taking away the-- the little children, the mothers.
[CRYING] I didn't have even a chance to say goodbye.
[CRYING] We just waved them.
And they're waving back.
And they told us that the women with the children, they are going to different areas.
And we were standing in lines, like soldiers.
And passing by, Mengele.
I didn't know who was Mengele.
But I saw a tall German and boots with a stick.
And he just, left, right.
And for some reason, he let me go with my father.
All my friends in my age-- not all of them, most of them-- went with their mother.
I went with my father.
So we were walking.
We were walking through this barbed-wire camp.
And we saw this strange-looking people coming close to the barbed wires and begging us that we should throw them whatever we have in our pockets.
And we didn't have too much because everything left in the boxcar.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but mainly Polish.
Some Hungarian, I heard, but mainly Polish and Slovakian.
I heard the languages.
And they were also yelling, give us whether you have left.
Because you won't need it anymore.
Things-- just crazy things.
But anyway, we walked to the-- towards a huge building.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And those were the showers.
Also, very fast.
Take off all your clothes.
Leave only the shoes and the belt, nothing else.
And then we were walking through some concrete floors.
A lot of-- it was wet all over and smelled the [INAUDIBLE] smelled from all over.
Very, very scary.
And my father hold me-- was holding my brother.
And my brother was holding me.
And we were walking.
And there were men standing with you know, the razor blades--
--and the machines.
And they cut the hairs.
And just one second, they cut my hair-- cut my hair, and quick into the showers.
But everything sounded so fast.
Everything, [GERMAN], this word.
So we went into the showers.
And they-- I don't know.
The-- the shower took about half a minute.
And then we again, lines.
And there were some large shelves there with those prisoners.
And they're throwing a shirt and a pants and a-- now, shoes we kept, I think.
Some of them, some of them.
If somebody had very beautiful, good boots or something, it was taken away right away and given this wooden boot.
So I was lucky.
I didn't have nothing special.
And a little boy, so nobody needed it.
But I-- I-- I kept my shoes and the belt, like I said.
And I was given a pants and a shirt.
And we were running into a big-- a huge room, probably a thousand people.
We were sitting.
And those crazy-behaved people, very few Germans.
We hardly saw German soldiers, one here, one there, mainly those kapos that I know, now-- but you didn't know-- were giving us orders.
Mainly that in-- in Auschwitz, I-- I-- I-- they were talking to us, Yiddish and Polish.
So I imagine they were Polish.
Perhaps--
